items.title: Audit trust scoring must not let dense graph edges mask missing context
items.summary: Fact: Kage audit now reports structured engineering memory coverage, code graph precision, memory code evidence, and a trust score. Why: Dense memory code graph edges can make a repository look well covered even when app
items.body:
Fact: Kage audit now reports structured engineering-memory coverage, code graph precision, memory-code evidence, and a trust score. Why: Dense memory-code graph edges can make a repository look well-covered even when approved memories lack explicit rationale, verification, or stale conditions. Trigger: When changing audit scoring, knowledge graph edge generation, or memory admission heuristics. Action: Cap memory-code edge scoring by approved packet count and prefer explicit Fact/Why/Action/Verified by/Stale when fields in high-value memories. Verified by: npm test in mcp; node mcp/dist/cli.js audit --project . --json. Risk if forgotten: Future audit changes may overstate repository trust and hide poor structured-memory coverage.
Evidence: Implemented structured context extraction, auditProject, memory-code graph evidence links, and a trust-score cap; tests passed and root audit reported trust_score 80 with structured-memory recommendations.
Verified by: npm test in mcp; node mcp/dist/cli.js audit --project . --json

items.title: Before You Edit risk page must explain why and what to do first
items.summary: The viewer Risks page was not user comprehensible when it exposed raw labels like blast radius, risk signals, silos, and generated memory packet paths. It now presents a Before You Edit checklist: filter generated memory
items.body:
The viewer Risks page was not user-comprehensible when it exposed raw labels like blast radius, risk signals, silos, and generated memory packet paths. It now presents a Before You Edit checklist: filter generated memory paths out of user-facing risk rows, show real code paths, explain why each area is risky, and include a concrete 'Do first' safety step. A related git status parsing bug trimmed modified paths from mcp/... to cp/... because gitLines already stripped leading status whitespace; gitChangedFiles now uses parsePorcelainPath.
Evidence: User reported they did not understand the risks or what to do. Headless viewer smoke showed Before You Edit page title/heading, rows with 'Why' and 'Do first', mcp/... paths, no agent_memory packet noise, and no body overflow. npm test --prefix mcp passed 173 tests; git diff --check passed.
Verified by: npm test --prefix mcp; git diff --check; local headless Chromium smoke against http://127.0.0.1:3128/

items.title: Code graph reuses per-file facts by content hash
items.summary: Kage code graph generation should cache per file extracted facts under .agent memory/code graph/file cache keyed by the file content hash. Unchanged files reuse cached file metadata, symbols, and imports; changed files m
items.body:
Kage code graph generation should cache per-file extracted facts under .agent_memory/code_graph/file-cache keyed by the file content hash. Unchanged files reuse cached file metadata, symbols, and imports; changed files miss the cache and regenerate facts. The cache is an internal performance layer, not durable memory, and global call edges still rebuild so cross-file relationships do not go stale. Manifest and metrics should expose cache_hits and cache_misses.
Evidence: Implemented in mcp/kernel.ts with FileCodeFacts cache helpers and CodeIndexManifest cache counters; covered by mcp/kernel.test.ts test 'code graph reuses per-file cached facts until source content changes'.
Verified by: npm test --prefix mcp

items.summary: Decision: The coding memory quality benchmark is now package callable, not only a source tree script. benchmarkCodingMemoryQuality lives in mcp/kernel.ts, kage benchmark memory quality exposes it through the CLI, and kag
items.body: Decision: The coding-memory quality benchmark is now package-callable, not only a source-tree script. benchmarkCodingMemoryQuality lives in mcp/kernel.ts, kage benchmark --memory-quality exposes it through the CLI, and kage_benchmark accepts mode=memory_quality for MCP users. items.title: Memory admission includes rationale, issues, and code explanations
items.summary: Fact: Kage memory admission must not use "would this change what a future agent does" as the only gate. Source backed rationale, bug causes, issue state, decisions, and non obvious code explanations are durable memory ev
items.body:
Fact: Kage memory admission must not use "would this change what a future agent does" as the only gate. Source-backed rationale, bug causes, issue state, decisions, and non-obvious code explanations are durable memory even when they primarily change what an agent understands before acting. Why: action-only phrasing discards exactly the context that prevents agents from undoing decisions, repeating bugs, or misreading code. Action: keep raw transcripts and routine command results episodic, but admit structured engineering context with provenance.
Evidence: User corrected the admission rule; implementation updated generated agent policy, admission docs, distillation heuristics, and tests for Code explanation and Issue context prompt observations.

items.title: Release workflow should be non-interactive and preflight remote state
items.summary: During the 1.1.14 npm release, git push was fast but the workflow slowed down because origin/master moved after publish, git rebase continue opened vi for .git/COMMIT EDITMSG, and helpful feedback recording after the rel
items.body:
During the 1.1.14 npm release, git push was fast but the workflow slowed down because origin/master moved after publish, git rebase --continue opened vi for .git/COMMIT_EDITMSG, and helpful-feedback recording after the release created a second memory commit. Future npm release work should run git fetch before publishing or pushing, use GIT_EDITOR=true or git -c core.editor=true for rebase/commit continuation in non-interactive agent sessions, and batch Kage feedback or memory writes before the final refresh/commit/push instead of adding them after the release push.
Evidence: Observed during @kage-core/kage-graph-mcp@1.1.14 release: first push rejected due remote update; rebase continuation left stale git rebase/vi processes; Kage feedback touched a packet after the release commit had already been pushed.

items.title: Tree-sitter tier sits between TS-AST and regex extraction
items.summary: Per file structural extraction in kernel.ts now has three tiers: TypeScript compiler AST for TS/JS, tree sitter web tree sitter WASM for python/go/rust/java/ruby, and the regex extractGenericSymbols/extractGenericCalls f
items.body:
Per-file structural extraction in kernel.ts now has three tiers: TypeScript compiler AST for TS/JS, tree-sitter (web-tree-sitter WASM) for python/go/rust/java/ruby, and the regex extractGenericSymbols/extractGenericCalls for everything else (kotlin, php, csharp, cpp, swift) or when a grammar fails to load. Because web-tree-sitter only initializes asynchronously while buildStructuralIndex/buildCodeGraph stay synchronous, grammars are loaded once per process before any file loop: cli.ts main() awaits ensureTreeSitterLanguages() after the help branch, index.ts callTool() awaits it first, and structural-worker.ts awaits it for the languages in its batch before mapping files. extractTreeSitterSymbols/extractTreeSitterCalls return null when no parser is loaded and callers fall back to the regex tier (?? extractGenericSymbols / ?? extractGenericCalls). Tree-sitter call edges use resolution "tree_sitter_name" with resolveCallTargets weights {local 0.8, imported 0.75, sameDir 0.55, nameOnly 0.32} — between regex (0.7) and TS-AST (0.9). usableStructuralCache treats cached generic-static symbols as a miss once the language's grammar is loaded so regex-tier cache entries cannot pin weak symbols past the upgrade. STRUCTURAL_EXTRACTOR_VERSION=4 and CODE_GRAPH_BUILDER_VERSION=3 force cache rebuilds.
Evidence: mcp/kernel.ts extractTreeSitterSymbols/extractTreeSitterCalls/usableStructuralCache; kernel.test.ts tree-sitter python and go tests.
Verified by: npm test (190 pass, 0 fail); pallets/click index: 1839/1839 symbols tree-sitter, 3704 calls tree_sitter_name

items.title: Viewer must not map file path aliases to arbitrary symbols
items.summary: Viewer merge aliases must not let a bare file path such as mcp/kernel.ts canonicalize to an arbitrary symbol in that file. Symbol and route code nodes should not advertise their file path as an alias; file paths belong t
items.body:
Viewer merge aliases must not let a bare file path such as mcp/kernel.ts canonicalize to an arbitrary symbol in that file. Symbol and route code nodes should not advertise their file path as an alias; file paths belong to file nodes. Otherwise many memory packets that mention a path can collapse onto the last symbol indexed for that path, creating bogus hubs such as byDate with thousands of memory-code edges. Exact file path memory can still become a memory-code link when the endpoint canonicalizes to a file node; broad paths use virtual affects_code_path bridge edges. Verified by npm --prefix mcp test and a viewer hook check showing byDate has only one defines_symbol edge.
Verified by: npm --prefix mcp test; viewer hook check

items.summary: The viewer's V2 "receipts" theme is light first warm paper tokens in :root with a dark palette under prefers color scheme: dark, in both mcp/viewer/index.html and docs/assets/site.css. Conventions future agents must foll
items.body:
The viewer's V2 "receipts" theme is light-first (warm paper tokens in :root) with a dark palette under prefers-color-scheme: dark, in both mcp/viewer/index.html and docs/assets/site.css. Conventions future agents must follow: (1) the daemon viewer URL is built by viewerUrl()/viewerReportPaths() in mcp/daemon.ts — add new report files there, never by string-appending; (2) reports.value (.agent_memory/reports/value.json) is the cumulative value ledger written by recall and must NEVER be regenerated/overwritten by startViewer's pre-generation block or all-time savings history is lost; (3) the Gains tab is the landing tab (show() defaults to "gains") and console.js recomputes today/7d windows client-side from ledger events with the same rules as `kage gains` (today = local midnight, all-time = totals so event-cap trimming never loses history, $15 per 1M input tokens); (4) the canvas memory map and donut cannot use CSS variables directly, so console.js resolves them via getComputedStyle into a THEME object (refreshTheme) and converts hex to rgba with the rgba() helper — use THEME.*, never hard-coded hex, so light/dark both work; (5) CSP discipline: index.html keeps exactly one external <script src="./console.js?v=N"> (bump N on changes, currently 16) and no inline scripts; (6) docs/viewer must stay a byte-identical mirror of mcp/viewer (pages.yml runs diff -qr) and pages.yml's optional-report loop now includes "value" so the hosted demo Gains tab populates from CI seed recalls.
Evidence: mcp/daemon.ts viewerReportPaths/viewerUrl; mcp/viewer/console.js renderGains/refreshTheme; mcp/daemon.test.ts "viewer url serves every report param including the value ledger"
Verified by: cd mcp && npm run build && npm test (196 pass); live viewer on :3199 served value.json and gains markup

items.title: web-tree-sitter must pair 0.24.x with tree-sitter-wasms and init once
items.summary: Kage's tree sitter extraction tier uses web tree sitter pure WASM, no node gyp with prebuilt grammars from tree sitter wasms. Two gotchas: 1 web tree sitter 0.25+/0.26 cannot load tree sitter wasms 0.1.x grammars built w
items.body:
Kage's tree-sitter extraction tier uses web-tree-sitter (pure WASM, no node-gyp) with prebuilt grammars from tree-sitter-wasms. Two gotchas: (1) web-tree-sitter 0.25+/0.26 cannot load tree-sitter-wasms 0.1.x grammars (built with tree-sitter-cli 0.20.8) — Language.load fails in getDylinkMetadata; the dependency must stay at web-tree-sitter ^0.24.7. (2) web-tree-sitter mutates its CommonJS export during Parser.init(), so a second require("web-tree-sitter") returns an object whose .init is not a function; the pre-init Parser class is cached once in kernel.ts (treeSitterRuntime) and reused for every Language.load. Per-language grammar npm packages (tree-sitter-python etc.) were rejected because they run node-gyp-build on install, which would break the npx install story.
Evidence: mcp/kernel.ts ensureTreeSitterLanguages; verified by loading python/go/rust/java/ruby grammars under web-tree-sitter 0.24.7 and reproducing both failure modes under 0.26.9 and double-require.
Verified by: npm test (190 pass) and acid index of pallets/click producing 1839 tree-sitter symbols

items.body:
Kage now writes a persisted local sparse-vector packet index at .agent_memory/indexes/vector-local.json during indexing and refresh. items.body:
Fact: Run the Kage MCP package test suite from the mcp directory with npm test, or from the repo root with npm test --prefix mcp. Why: The test script builds TypeScript first and then runs node --test over dist/**/*.test.js, so it verifies both compilation and behavior. Trigger: Recall when asked how to run tests, verify recall/ranking changes, or check MCP package behavior. Risk if forgotten: Agents may cite old release proof memories that mention tests without giving the actual command.
Evidence: mcp/package.json defines test as npm run build && node --test dist/**/*.test.js.

items.title: Stale-catch: change-time invalidation heartbeat (staleCatch, kage staleguard)
items.summary: Stale catch is the change time retention heartbeat: staleCatch projectDir, changedFiles? in mcp/kernel.ts intersects working tree changes git diff HEAD + staged + untracked, or an explicit file list with packet path fing
items.body: Stale-catch is the change-time retention heartbeat: staleCatch(projectDir, changedFiles?) in mcp/kernel.ts intersects working-tree changes (git diff HEAD + staged + untracked, or an explicit file list) with packet path_fingerprints and reports each mismatch as {packet_title, packet_id, cited_path, reason}. Every catch records a value-ledger event kind stale_caught (totals + window summaries, shown by kage gains as 'stale caught at change-time'). Surfaces: kage pr check prints the human summary first, kage staleguard is the advisory exit-0 pre-commit/pre-push variant, and the kage_pr_check MCP tool prepends the same lines via formatStaleCatch so agents relay it. The ValueEvent union, ledger totals, and ValueWindowSummary all gained stale_caught; extend all of them together when adding a new ledger kind.
